images.caption: Figure 1:Pass@32 performance on MiniF2F-Test, PutnamBench, and MathOlympiadBench. OProver consistently outperforms prior open-source whole-proof provers across all three benchmarks; the only exception is LongCat-Flash-Prover with TIR on MathOlympiadBench.

Figure 2:Overview of OProver. The framework has three components:OProofs Construction(top-left), which builds a Lean-specific corpus from public Lean resources and autoformalized statements;OProver Agentic Proving(top-right), which performs multi-round refinement under retrieval and Lean 4 compiler feedback; andOProver Agentic Training(bottom), where a one-time CPT yields OProver-Base, followed by an iterative post-training loop in which agentic proving, SFT, and RL produceOProvert+1\text{OProver}_{t+1}fromOProvert\text{OProver}_{t}, while verified proofs are folded back into OProofs.

Figure 4:Test-time scaling of OProver under a fixed total budgetBB. For each benchmark, we reportBestPass​(B)\mathrm{BestPass}(B)(defined in §4.2), the best success rate achievable under budgetBBacross all(R,k)(R,k)allocations. Both OProver-8B and OProver-32B improve consistently with larger budgets, while the gains exhibit clear diminishing returns.

Figure 5:Performance under fixed total budgetBBas a function of refinement depthRR. Each curve plotsPass​(R,B/R)\mathrm{Pass}(R,B/R), isolating the trade-off between deeper interaction and wider sampling. On most benchmarks performance keeps improving asRRgrows up toR=16R=16, while on the hardest benchmark (PutnamBench) the optimum settles aroundR=8R=8, indicating that very narrow sampling can hurt when single-chain success probability is low.
Figure 6:MiniF2F-Test Pass@32 across post-training iterations. Both OProver-8B and OProver-32B improve monotonically.
